title: Risk Management
summary: Identifying, analysing, and responding to threats and opportunities so uncertainty is managed rather than merely feared. In PMBOK 8 this is the Risk performance domain, supported by the principle Adopt a Holistic View: optimise responses to maximise opportunity and minimise threat.
intro: The full risk loop, threat/opportunity strategies, reserves and EMV.

sections.heading: The Risk performance domain
sections.body:
A risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has an effect on one or more project objectives. That effect can be negative (a threat) or positive (an opportunity) - PMI treats both as risk, so good risk management is not only about avoiding harm but also about capturing upside.

PMBOK 8 frames all of this as the Risk performance domain (desired outcome: threats reduced, opportunities captured, the environment understood). It is one of the seven performance domains, and it draws on the principle Adopt a Holistic View - seeing risk and complexity in the context of the whole system, so responses are chosen to fit context rather than applied by rote.

blocks.title: How PMBOK 8 sees risk
lines: Risk is one of the seven performance domains (Governance, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Stakeholders, Resources, Risk).
The domain's aim: reduce threats, capture opportunities, understand the environment.
It connects to the principle Adopt a Holistic View, under which risk and complexity sit; there is no standalone risk principle.
Responses are tailored and proportionate to priority - both threats (downside) and opportunities (upside) are managed as risk.

sections.heading: The risk process, end to end
sections.body: Risk management is iterative: it runs throughout the project, not just at the start, because new risks emerge as work progresses and old ones expire.

blocks.title: Plan -> Identify -> Analyse -> Respond -> Implement -> Monitor

items.title: Plan risk management
lines: Decide how risk will be handled on this project.
Set categories, scales and tolerances up front.

items.title: Identify risks
lines: Iterative and team-driven; continues throughout.
Use the RBS to prompt systematic identification.
Record each risk in the risk register.

items.title: Analyse - qualitative
lines: Quick, subjective scoring to prioritise.
Rate probability and impact; plot on the P x I matrix.

items.title: Analyse - quantitative
lines: Numeric modelling of overall exposure.
EMV, decision trees, Monte Carlo, sensitivity analysis.
Often skipped on smaller or agile projects.

items.title: Plan responses
lines: Choose a strategy per risk, proportionate to priority.
Assign a risk owner.
Check for secondary risk the response creates.

items.title: Implement responses
lines: Carry out the agreed response actions.
A known risk that fires triggers its planned response.

items.title: Monitor risks
lines: Track identified risks, watch for new ones, review reserves.
Continues to the end of the project.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: A new risk: record it, analyse it, plan a response, assign an owner.
A known risk fires: use the planned response - don't improvise.
Identification never stops; keep the register live.

sections.heading: Identifying risks and the Risk Breakdown Structure
sections.body: Risk identification is iterative and team-driven. The Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) is a hierarchical chart of risk categories - not individual risks - used to prompt systematic identification so nothing obvious is missed.

rows.value: Requirements, technology, complexity, interfaces, quality.
Suppliers, regulation, market, weather.
Resources, funding, priorities, dependencies.
Estimating, planning, controlling, communication.

blocks.title: Don't confuse the structures
blocks.text: The RBS organises risk by SOURCE / category; the WBS organises deliverable work. They are different breakdowns.

sections.heading: Qualitative analysis - the probability and impact matrix
sections.body:
Qualitative analysis rapidly scores and ranks each identified risk so attention goes to the high-priority few. You rate probability and impact (typically high / medium / low or a numeric scale) and multiply or plot them on a probability and impact matrix, often colour-coded red / amber / green.

The output is a prioritised list: red risks demand response now, green risks may go on a watch list. It is fast and subjective - it relies on expert judgement rather than hard numbers. Qualitative analysis usually decides which risks warrant deeper quantitative analysis or an immediate response, and which can simply be monitored.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='70' y='30' width='60' height='46' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.35' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='130' y='30' width='60' height='46' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.38' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='190' y='30' width='60' height='46' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.38' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='70' y='76' width='60' height='46' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.32' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='130' y='76' width='60' height='46' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.35' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='190' y='76' width='60' height='46' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.38' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='70' y='122' width='60' height='46' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.32' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='130' y='122' width='60' height='46' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.32' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><rect x='190' y='122' width='60' height='46' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.35' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.5'/><text x='62' y='56' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>High</text><text x='62' y='102' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>Med</text><text x='62' y='148' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>Low</text><text x='30' y='99' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 30 99)'>Probability</text><text x='100' y='183' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Low</text><text x='160' y='183' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Med</text><text x='220' y='183' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>High</text><text x='160' y='199' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Impact</text></svg>
figure.caption: Probability x impact: red cells (top-right) are highest priority; green (bottom-left) a watch list.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Qualitative = quick, subjective, prioritises. Think 'which risks matter most?' before spending money modelling them.

blocks.title: Reading the P x I matrix

rows.value: Top-right cells; highest priority - respond now.
Mid cells; plan a response or monitor closely.
Bottom-left cells; a watch list may be enough.

sections.heading: Quantitative analysis - EMV and decision trees
sections.body:
Quantitative analysis is the numeric side: it assigns money or time figures to risk to model overall project exposure. Expected Monetary Value (EMV) is probability times impact, expressed in money - negative for threats, positive for opportunities. Summing EMVs gives an expected contingency figure.

Decision trees extend EMV across choices: each branch's EMV is calculated and you select the path with the best overall EMV. Other quantitative techniques include Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis (tornado diagrams). Quantitative analysis is effort-intensive and is often skipped on smaller or agile projects, where qualitative ranking and adaptive responses are enough.

lines: Expressed in money.
Threats carry a minus sign; opportunities a plus.
Sum identified risks to size contingency.
A 20% chance of a -50,000 threat.
Decision tree: pick the branch with the best overall EMV.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Threats are negative EMV; opportunities are positive.
Monte Carlo and sensitivity (tornado) analysis are also quantitative.
Small / agile projects often stop at qualitative ranking.

sections.heading: Risk responses - threats and opportunities
sections.body: Each strategy for a threat has a mirror for an opportunity. Choose responses proportionate to the risk's priority - optimise, don't over- or under-respond.

rows.value: Eliminate the threat entirely or remove its cause, e.g. change scope or approach.
Shift the impact to a third party via insurance, contracts or warranties; the risk still exists, ownership of its consequence moves.
Reduce the probability and/or the impact to an acceptable level.
Take no proactive action. Active acceptance sets aside a contingency reserve; passive acceptance simply deals with it if it occurs.
Threat is outside the project's authority, so raise it to programme, portfolio or senior management.
Make certain the opportunity happens (the mirror of avoid).
Allocate it to a partner best able to capture it, e.g. a joint venture or teaming agreement (mirror of transfer).
Increase its probability and/or impact (mirror of mitigate).
Take it if it arrives but don't actively pursue it.
Beyond the project's remit, so raise it upward.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Transfer and Share both involve a third party.
Escalate and Accept are the two strategies shared by threats and opportunities.
Avoid <-> Exploit, Mitigate <-> Enhance, Transfer <-> Share are the mirror pairs.

sections.heading: Secondary and residual risk, and the bowtie
sections.body: Applying a response is not the end of the story - it can leave risk behind and can create new risk.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='34' y1='44' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='34' y1='80' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='34' y1='116' x2='148' y2='80' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='44' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='80' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='172' y1='80' x2='286' y2='116' stroke='#C98A1F' stroke-width='1.1'/><line x1='104' y1='36' x2='104' y2='124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><line x1='216' y1='36' x2='216' y2='124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><circle cx='160' cy='80' r='18' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.15' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='160' y='83' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Event</text><text x='32' y='30' fill='#B5462F' font-size='9'>Threats</text><text x='288' y='30' fill='#C98A1F' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>Consequences</text><text x='104' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>preventive</text><text x='216' y='140' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>recovery</text></svg>
figure.caption: Bowtie: preventive barriers stop the causes (left); recovery barriers limit the consequences (right).

groups.label: What it is
lines: The exposure that REMAINS after a response is applied.
You mitigated, but some risk is left over.

groups.label: What you do
lines: It is expected; then accepted or watched.

groups.label: What it is
lines: A NEW risk CREATED BY the response itself.
E.g. transferring work to a subcontractor creates a delivery-quality risk.

groups.label: What you do
lines: Always check what a response introduces.

blocks.title: The bowtie method
lines: Visualises a serious risk: the central knot is the unwanted event.
Threats (causes) on the left; consequences on the right.
Preventive barriers stop the causes (left); recovery barriers limit the consequences (right).
Most useful for high-severity risks where you want prevention and mitigation at a glance.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Residual = left over after the response; Secondary = caused by the response. Bowtie: prevent on the left, recover on the right.

sections.heading: Reserves - contingency vs management
sections.body: Two reserves cover two different kinds of uncertainty.

rows.value: Contingency: KNOWN risks (identified 'known-unknowns'). Management: UNKNOWN risks (the 'unknown-unknowns' you could not foresee).
Contingency: part of the cost baseline. Management: OUTSIDE the cost baseline, within the total project budget.
Contingency: PM can spend without further approval. Management: requires management approval to access.

blocks.title: Exam cue
blocks.text: Known + in baseline + PM controls = contingency. Unknown + outside baseline + needs approval = management reserve.

sections.heading: Risk in adaptive and agile work
sections.body:
In adaptive projects, risk management is not a one-time planning activity. The team looks for uncertainty in every iteration, planning event, review, and retrospective, then adjusts work before exposure grows.

The PMP exam may frame this as agile, hybrid, iterative, or incremental delivery. The core idea is the same: short feedback cycles make risk easier to see, discuss, and respond to.
sections.tip: For the exam, connect agile risk management to transparency, frequent inspection, and adaptation. It does not remove the need for a risk register or clear ownership.

items.title: Inspect
lines: Review new information from demos, tests, stakeholder feedback, and team conversations.
Look for both threats and opportunities.

items.title: Reassess
lines: Update probability, impact, urgency, and proximity as conditions change.
Retire risks that no longer matter and add newly discovered risks.

items.title: Adapt
lines: Change backlog order, response actions, reserves, or stakeholder communication based on current exposure.
Use the next iteration to reduce uncertainty where possible.

sections.heading: Risk-adjusted backlog prioritization
sections.body:
A backlog can be prioritized by value, dependencies, urgency, and risk. Risk-adjusted ordering brings uncertain, complex, or technically difficult items forward so the team can learn early instead of discovering major problems near the end.

This does not mean every risky item is always first. The product owner and team balance risk reduction against value delivery, stakeholder priorities, and capacity.

groups.label: What
lines: The team lacks enough information to estimate, design, or test confidently.

groups.label: Use
lines: Run a spike, prototype, experiment, or proof of concept.

groups.label: What
lines: Failure could harm scope, schedule, cost, quality, compliance, or customer value.

groups.label: Use
lines: Escalate visibility, assign an owner, and plan a response before commitment.

groups.label: What
lines: Early work can validate assumptions and reduce later rework.

groups.label: Use
lines: Schedule the item early enough for results to influence future planning.

sections.heading: Risk ownership and collaboration
sections.body:
A risk without an owner is unlikely to be managed well. Ownership means one person is accountable for tracking the risk, coordinating the response, and reporting changes in exposure.

In agile and hybrid environments, ownership still matters, but risk identification and response are collaborative. Developers, testers, customers, vendors, business owners, and the project manager may all notice different sources of uncertainty.
sections.tip: On exam questions, do not choose an answer where the project manager silently handles all risk alone. PMBOK favors engagement, accountability, and transparent communication.

rows.value: Accountable for monitoring a specific risk and driving the agreed response.
Responsible for completing a particular response action, which may be different from the risk owner.
Facilitates the process, maintains visibility, removes barriers, and escalates when needed.
Help identify risks, validate assumptions, and contribute practical response ideas.

sections.heading: Visualizing risk work
sections.body:
Visual tools make risk easier to discuss and harder to ignore. A team may use a risk board, colored backlog items, a risk register view, or a risk burndown chart to show current exposure and response progress.

The tool is less important than the behavior it supports: shared visibility, timely decisions, and follow-through on response actions.

groups.label: What
lines: A visible workflow for risks, such as identified, analyzed, response planned, monitoring, and closed.

groups.label: Use
lines: Supports daily or iteration-level discussion of active risks.

groups.label: What
lines: A trend view of risk exposure or risk count over time.

groups.label: Use
lines: Shows whether responses are reducing exposure as the project progresses.

groups.label: What
lines: Backlog items marked by relative risk level.

groups.label: Use
lines: Helps the team discuss risk during refinement, planning, and sequencing.

sections.heading: Risk appetite, tolerance, and thresholds
sections.body:
Risk appetite describes how much uncertainty an organization is willing to pursue in exchange for value. Risk tolerance is the acceptable variation for a specific objective, stakeholder, or project area. A threshold is the point at which action, escalation, or governance is required.

These concepts help the team decide whether to accept, respond to, escalate, or avoid a risk. The same exposure may be acceptable in one organization and unacceptable in another.

items.text: The general degree of risk an organization is willing to take to create value.
The acceptable range of variation around a specific objective, such as budget, schedule, or performance.
A defined trigger point that requires action, escalation, or a change in response.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: When a risk exceeds an agreed threshold, the best answer usually involves escalation, replanning, or an approved response rather than informal acceptance.

sections.heading: Learning from risk responses
sections.body:
Risk management improves when the team studies what happened, not just what was planned. Retrospectives, lessons learned, incident reviews, and risk audits help the team judge whether responses were timely, proportionate, and effective.

The goal is not blame. The goal is better prediction, stronger response choices, and reusable knowledge for the current project and future work.

items.title: What changed
lines: Which risks increased, decreased, occurred, or disappeared during the last period?

items.title: What worked
lines: Which responses reduced probability, impact, or urgency as expected?

items.title: What to improve
lines: Which assumptions were wrong, which triggers were missed, and what should be updated in the risk register or backlog?

source: PM Cram study notes - restructured into blocks and aligned to PMBOK 8 (Risk performance domain).
